nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing, well-oxygenated river channels, rapids, and the mouths of tributary streams; mid-water to surface in main river channels and rocky areas with strong current

decorPreferences: minimal decor to maximize open swimming space
smooth rounded boulders or large river-worn stones
robust driftwood for structure without obstruction
strong directional flow from powerful filtration or powerheads
very tightly sealed lid — powerful jumper

diet.notes: Obligate piscivore in the wild — feeds primarily on other fish including piranhas, using massive lower-jaw fangs (up to 6 cm) to impale prey. In captivity, weaning onto non-live food is extremely difficult; most specimens will only reliably accept live feeder fish long-term. Some individuals can be transitioned to large chunks of fresh whole fish (smelt, whitebait, tilapia pieces), large earthworms, and occasionally frozen whole fish over time. Prepared pellets and sticks are rarely accepted. Vitamin supplementation advisable if feeding feeder fish exclusively. Gut-loaded or quarantined feeders reduce disease risk.

goodWith: large, robust, similarly-sized fish too large to be eaten (as tankmates only in very large public-aquarium setups)
large doradid catfish (bottom dwellers that stay out of mid-water)
large armored catfish (Pseudoplatystoma, Brachyplatystoma) in specialist setups
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be eaten (effectively all community fish)
piranhas and other aggressive fish at similar size (mutual predation or injury)
conspecifics except in very large groups in public-aquarium scale tanks
any fish without fast-flowing, high-oxygen conditions

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Hydrolycus scomberoides showing characteristic fangs and body form.

summary: The Payara is a massive Amazonian piscivore reaching 60–100 cm, famous for enormous lower-jaw fangs that fit into pits in the upper jaw. An obligate live-fish feeder almost impossible to wean; requires 1000 L+, very high O2, and strong flow. Public aquarium or highly specialist use only.
careNotes: The Payara (Hydrolycus scomberoides) is one of the most dramatic — and most demanding — large predatory characins available to the hobby. Wild fish inhabit fast-flowing, highly oxygenated stretches of the Amazon and Orinoco river systems, hunting other fish with a pair of elongated fang-like teeth on the lower jaw that can reach 6 cm in length; these teeth slot into corresponding pits or holes in the upper jaw when the mouth is closed. The species is capable of taking prey items as large as adult piranhas. Minimum tank size of 1000 L is a firm floor, with footprints of 240 cm+ essential for a single adult. Oxygen saturation must be maintained near the maximum for the temperature range: strong surface agitation, a powerful sump return, or dedicated powerheads pointed toward the surface are all necessary. Temperature should remain in the cooler portion of the tropically-appropriate range (22–26°C is preferred; sustained temperatures above 28°C cause rapid deterioration). Water chemistry should be soft and slightly acidic to neutral, reflecting Amazonian river conditions; high hardness or alkalinity is poorly tolerated. The species is extremely skittish and prone to violent escape attempts — a completely sealed and weighted lid is non-negotiable. Specimens in captivity frequently injure themselves by running into glass at speed; black or heavily opaque sides can help reduce startle responses. Keeping with tankmates is not generally recommended for home aquaria; even large fish may be attacked. Trade specimens are almost always wild-caught. Taxonomy note: the Hydrolycus genus contains four species — H. scomberoides, H. armatus (larger, fangs proportionally even longer), H. tatauaia, and H. wallacei — that are frequently misidentified in the trade, with H. armatus in particular often sold as Payara. H. scomberoides has two fang pits in the upper jaw, while H. armatus typically has one; both may appear in the hobby under the same common names.
breedingNotes: No confirmed captive breeding records exist for Hydrolycus scomberoides. The species is assumed to be a broadcast spawner in the wild, likely undertaking migrations to specific spawning sites along river corridors. The conditions required — space, flow, and seasonal water-chemistry cues — cannot be replicated in home or most public aquarium settings. All trade specimens are wild-caught.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hydrolycus scomberoides
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 117 cm TL), temperature (22–27°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (2–12 dH), native range (Amazon and Orinoco basins), habitat (fast-flowing channels, rapids), diet (piscivore — live fish in captivity), compatibility, and detailed husbandry notes including high-oxygen requirement, skittishness, and tank-size guidance.

additional.name: FishBase — Hydrolycus scomberoides
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (117 cm TL), native range (Amazon, Orinoco, and associated drainages), taxonomy (Cynodontidae, Characiformes), diet (fish), and temperature tolerance. Confirmed species is distinct from H. armatus and H. tatauaia.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Vampire fish: Hydrolycus scomberoides
additional.notes: Supplemented husbandry details: high-flow and high-oxygen requirements, live-feeding difficulty, escape risk, genus confusion in the trade (H. armatus vs H. scomberoides), and note that fang pits distinguish the two most common trade species. Recommended tank size 1000 L+ for adults.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Payara (Vampire Tetra) Care Guide
additional.notes: Provided hobbyist-oriented care summary: temperature 72–82°F (22–28°C), pH 6.0–7.5, minimum 250 gallons (~950 L) for adults, carnivore/live feeder diet, expert care level, escape risk warning. Broadly consistent with SeriouslyFish data; used to cross-check tank volume lower bound and temperature range.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Payara: The Vampire Fish of the Amazon
additional.notes: Historical trade account; confirmed fang anatomy (lower-jaw teeth slot into upper-jaw pits), wild diet including piranhas, skittish behavior, and that H. armatus is frequently confused with H. scomberoides in the hobby. Noted short captive lifespan common without correct high-O2 conditions.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records. All trade specimens are wild-caught. Assumed broadcast spawner requiring migratory cues and river-corridor conditions impossible to replicate in captivity.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 60+ cm; needs 1000 L+ high-O2 flowing tanks and a live-fish-only diet. Specialist or public-aquarium setups only.
